stem: A score of \( [[xval]] \) comes from a distribution with mean \( [[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( [[sigma]] \). What is the z-score of this value?
solution: The z-score measures how many standard deviations the value is from the mean: \( z = \frac{[[xval]] - [[mu]]}{[[sigma]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the subtraction, giving the opposite sign of the correct z-score.
This is the raw deviation from the mean; a z-score also divides by the standard deviation.
This divides by the mean instead of by the standard deviation.
This is off by one from the correct z-score.

stem: A distribution has mean \( [[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( [[sigma]] \). What raw value has a z-score of \( [[z]] \)?
solution: A raw value is the mean plus the z-score times the standard deviation: \( x = [[mu]] + [[z]]\cdot[[sigma]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts instead of adds, giving the value on the opposite side of the mean.
This reports only the deviation from the mean and forgets to add the mean itself.
This adds the z-score directly to the mean and forgets to scale it by the standard deviation.
This multiplies all three quantities together instead of adding the scaled deviation to the mean.
This is off by one from the correct raw value.

stem: Scores on a test are approximately normal with mean \( [[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( [[sigma]] \). Two students score \( [[x1]] \) and \( [[x2]] \), respectively. By how much does the first student's z-score exceed the second student's z-score?
solution: Since both scores share the same mean and standard deviation, the mean cancels in the difference of z-scores: \( \frac{[[x1]]-[[mu]]}{[[sigma]]} - \frac{[[x2]]-[[mu]]}{[[sigma]]} = \frac{[[x1]]-[[x2]]}{[[sigma]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign, giving the second student's z-score minus the first's.
This divides by the mean instead of by the standard deviation.
This is the raw difference in scores, without dividing by the standard deviation.
This reports only the first student's z-score, forgetting to subtract the second's.
This is off by one from the correct difference.

stem: Two students each take a different exam. The first exam has mean \( [[mu1]] \) and standard deviation \( [[sigma1]] \); that student scores \( [[x1]] \). The second exam has mean \( [[mu2]] \) and standard deviation \( [[sigma2]] \); that student scores \( [[x2]] \). By how much does the first student's z-score exceed the second student's z-score?
solution: Standardize each score against its OWN exam's mean and standard deviation, then subtract: \( \dfrac{[[x1]]-[[mu1]]}{[[sigma1]]} - \dfrac{[[x2]]-[[mu2]]}{[[sigma2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign, giving the second student's z-score minus the first's.
This treats the two exams as if they shared a single combined standard deviation, rather than standardizing each separately.
This reports only the first student's z-score, forgetting to subtract the second's.
This reports only the second student's z-score, forgetting to subtract it from the first's.
This is off by one from the correct difference.
